content.text: Milestone gate reviews are formal, time-boxed checkpoints placed at pre-defined project milestones where an independent panel evaluates whether a project meets documented exit criteria before authorising it to proceed to the next stage. They are distinct from periodic progress reviews: the gate is a binary decision point — proceed, proceed with conditions, pause for remediation, or kill — not a status update.

A milestone gate review is a structured checkpoint that separates the phases of a project. Entry to a review is conditional on the delivery team first producing a gate package — a documented set of evidence that demonstrates the work completed in the preceding phase. The review panel, composed of independent reviewers who are not members of the delivery team, evaluates the package against pre-agreed exit criteria and records a formal outcome.

The six standard gates for a GenAI delivery programme are: Gate 0 (Concept approved), Gate 1 (Feasibility confirmed), Gate 2 (Design complete), Gate 3 (Pilot done), Gate 4 (Production-ready), Gate 5 (Value achieved). Each gate has its own entry criteria, exit criteria, required deliverables, and designated reviewer roles. The gate sequence enforces a discipline that prevents teams from proceeding with unresolved design flaws, unmet data quality requirements, or unapproved security postures.

Without formal gate reviews, organisations repeatedly discover critical problems late — after significant investment — when correction is expensive or the project must be abandoned. Common failure modes include: launching a GenAI feature before a privacy impact assessment is complete, discovering hallucination rates unacceptable for the use case only after production rollout, and discovering integration failures between the AI component and downstream systems during production incidents rather than in a pilot.

Gate reviews create a forcing function that surfaces these problems at the cheapest point to fix them. A pharmaceutical company that introduced a 5-gate review process reduced post-launch product failures by 40% because quality, regulatory, and safety gaps were caught at Gate 3 (Pilot Done) rather than after approval and commercial launch, when remediation costs are orders of magnitude higher.
Critical flaws caught at gates 1–2 cost 10–50x less to fix than the same flaws caught in production.
Independent review creates a clear audit trail showing who approved what and on what evidence.
Kill decisions at early gates prevent continued investment in fundamentally unviable initiatives.
Sponsors and regulators can see the structured evidence that supported approval decisions.
Gate status across a portfolio provides programme leadership with objective project health signals.
Gate packages take time to prepare. Teams must budget 2–5 days per gate for documentation.
Gates conducted as rubber-stamp exercises by reviewers who lack domain depth provide no value.
Teams may define weak exit criteria to ensure passage. Criteria must be set before the project starts, not by the delivery team.
If multiple projects reach the same gate simultaneously, reviewer capacity becomes a constraint that must be managed.
The gate review process has three phases: preparation, review, and recording.

In preparation (1–2 weeks before the gate), the delivery team assembles the gate package: deliverables checklist (all artefacts produced in the phase), quality scorecard (metrics against acceptance thresholds), updated risk register, and budget actuals versus plan. The package is distributed to reviewers no fewer than 5 business days before the scheduled review.

The review itself is time-boxed to a maximum of 2 hours. The panel does not re-litigate scope or reopen resolved design decisions — it evaluates evidence against exit criteria. Each exit criterion is scored pass, partial, or fail. If the aggregate score is below the threshold, the panel agrees on a remediation plan with a specific re-gate date or votes to kill.

After the review, outcomes are recorded in the gate review record (typically in Confluence) within 24 hours. Jira automation creates the next phase's epic and assigns remediation tickets if a conditional approval or pause outcome was reached.

attrs.alt: Milestone Gate Review Pipeline: six gates (Concept through Value Achieved) with four decision branches at each gate (Proceed, Proceed with Conditions, Pause/Remediate, Kill) and gate review panel composition

content.text: The pipeline runs left to right through six gates. At each gate the delivery team presents their package to an independent panel. The panel's decision determines whether the project continues to the next gate, returns to remediation with a re-gate date, or is stopped. The panel composition varies by gate: early gates (0–1) require business sponsor and AI lead; middle gates (2–3) require architect, security, and QA; late gates (4–5) require ops, compliance, and finance.
GenAI feature programmes with regulatory exposure (financial services, healthcare, pharma) where gate sign-off forms part of the audit trail.
Projects with a budget above the organisation's capital expenditure threshold requiring formal appropriation at defined milestones.
Cross-functional programmes involving legal, compliance, security, and multiple engineering teams where informal coordination is insufficient.
Platform initiatives where downstream teams depend on agreed-quality deliverables to proceed in parallel.
Any project where a previous similar initiative failed late and cost over-runs were significant.

Define exit criteria for all gates before the project starts, in a gate criteria configuration file. Exit criteria must be specific, measurable, and agreed by the gate panel members in advance. The configuration below shows a GenAI feature gate setup.

# gate-criteria.yaml  —  GenAI Feature gate configuration
gates:
  - id: G0
    name: Concept
    entry_criteria:
      - Business problem statement approved by sponsor
      - High-level use case documented
    exit_criteria:
      - ROI hypothesis accepted (>20% efficiency gain OR >$500K NPV)
      - Data availability confirmed with data owners
      - No blocking regulatory objections identified
    max_review_duration_minutes: 60
    required_reviewers: [business_sponsor, ai_lead]

  - id: G1
    name: Feasibility
    exit_criteria:
      - Technical feasibility study complete (LLM/RAG/agent selection justified)
      - Data quality score >= 80 on 3+ representative samples
      - Privacy impact assessment approved by DPO
      - Estimated build cost within 30% of initial estimate
    required_reviewers: [architect, risk_officer, business_sponsor]

  - id: G2
    name: Design Complete
    exit_criteria:
      - System design document approved by architecture board
      - Security threat model reviewed and mitigations accepted
      - Model evaluation framework defined with baseline metrics
      - Test plan covering safety, bias, and hallucination approved
    required_reviewers: [architect, security_lead, qa_lead, business_sponsor]

  - id: G3
    name: Pilot Done
    exit_criteria:
      - Pilot with >= 50 real users completed
      - Primary success metric >= 80% of target
      - Safety / hallucination rate < 2%
      - No P1 defects open; P2 defects <= 3 with accepted mitigations
    required_reviewers: [product_owner, qa_lead, risk_officer, business_sponsor]

  - id: G4
    name: Production-Ready
    exit_criteria:
      - All P1/P2 defects resolved
      - Load test: P99 latency < 2s at 10x expected load
      - Runbook and rollback plan approved by ops
      - Regulatory sign-off obtained (if required)
      - Monitoring dashboards live and alerting verified
    required_reviewers: [ops_lead, security_lead, compliance, business_sponsor]

  - id: G5
    name: Value Achieved
    exit_criteria:
      - Business KPIs measured over >= 90 days in production
      - Primary metric within 10% of committed target
      - Cost-per-transaction within approved range
      - User satisfaction score >= 4.0 / 5.0
    required_reviewers: [business_sponsor, finance, product_owner]

Store this file in the project repository. Reference it in the gate review record template in Confluence. Jira automation should create a gate review epic at project kick-off and auto-transition sub-tasks when criteria are marked complete. A GitHub Actions workflow can post a gate readiness summary to the Slack channel when the delivery team marks the gate package as ready for review.

A retail bank's GenAI virtual assistant programme used a 6-gate structure. At Gate 1 (Feasibility), the panel found that the preferred LLM provider could not meet the bank's data residency requirement — all inference had to occur within the EU. The gate was failed, a compliant provider was selected, and the project re-gated 3 weeks later. This 3-week delay prevented a 6-month regulatory remediation that would have been required had the issue been discovered post-launch.

An insurance firm's claims summarisation project reached Gate 3 (Pilot Done) with a hallucination rate of 4.2% — above the agreed 2% threshold. The gate outcome was Pause for Remediation. The team implemented retrieval-augmented generation with source citation, reducing the rate to 1.1% in 6 weeks. The project proceeded through Gate 4 and Gate 5 without further gate failures.

A global pharmaceutical company reduced post-launch product failures by 40% after implementing a structured 5-gate review process across its AI-assisted drug development programmes. Previously, AI models used to predict clinical trial dropout risk were deployed to production before full validation against the trial dataset, leading to biased predictions that skewed resource allocation. The retrospective root cause was that no formal gate existed between development completion and production deployment.

After implementing the gate framework, Gate 4 (Production-Ready) required validated model performance across all demographic sub-groups at or above the agreed threshold (AUC >= 0.80 in each subgroup) and independent review by a biostatistician who was not on the development team. Two subsequent projects were held at Gate 4 — one for 3 weeks pending sub-group validation, one killed when sub-group performance could not be improved. The programme director credited the gate framework with preventing two post-launch withdrawals estimated to cost $12M in remediation and regulatory penalty.
Setting exit criteria after the work is done:
Criteria defined retrospectively are weak and often shaped to match the work rather than the business need.
Including delivery team members on the review panel:
The team has an inherent interest in passing the gate; independence is non-negotiable.
Allowing gate reviews to run over 2 hours:
Long reviews signal insufficient preparation. If a panel cannot reach a decision in 2 hours, the gate package was inadequate.
Recording only the outcome, not the reasoning:
Future auditors need to understand why the decision was made, not just what it was.
Teams treat production launch as the endpoint. Without a formal value gate, organisations never close the loop between promised and delivered business outcomes.
Appoint a gate secretary who is not a reviewer — they run the timer, take minutes, and own the gate record within 24 hours.
Use a fixed gate package template: a project that deviates from the template has almost certainly not addressed all required areas.
Include at least one domain expert who is not from IT — a clinical lead for healthcare AI, a credit risk officer for financial AI.
Set numerical thresholds in exit criteria wherever possible (hallucination rate < 2%, latency P99 < 2s). Qualitative criteria are easy to argue over; numbers are not.
Require a 5-day package distribution window before the review — panels that read the package in the meeting do not have time to identify material gaps.
Run a brief pre-gate readiness check with the delivery lead 1 week before the gate to identify any package gaps while there is still time to address them.

The gate framework itself adds no compute overhead, but the process overhead scales with programme count. At 5 or more concurrent GenAI programmes, reviewer capacity becomes the binding constraint. Mitigation approaches include: maintaining a standing reviewer pool with defined availability commitments, scheduling gates on a fixed monthly cadence (all Gate 3 reviews on the second Thursday of each month), and using asynchronous pre-read scoring to reduce synchronous review time.

Gate package preparation time decreases as teams build familiarity with the format — expect 3–5 days per gate on first delivery, falling to 1–2 days by the third project. Automating the quality scorecard (pulling metrics directly from dashboards and test reports) reduces manual compilation effort.
Gate review record templates, exit criteria pages, and gate package checklists stored as Confluence page templates for consistent documentation.
Gate review epics with sub-tasks per exit criterion; automation rules that transition gate status and create remediation tickets on conditional approval.
Automated gate readiness checks that parse test result files and flag whether measurable exit criteria are met.
Portfolio gate dashboards showing which projects are at which gate, how long they have been at that gate, and the number of conditional approvals.
In regulated industries, gate records can be managed as change requests with full approval workflow and audit trail built in.
Mature software with high test coverage

Gate records must be tamper-evident. Store gate review records in a system that logs all edits with authorship and timestamp — Confluence provides this via its page history. For regulated industries, gate approvals may need to be cryptographically signed (e.g., using DocuSign for the gate sign-off) to meet electronic records requirements (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, EMA Annex 11).

Gate packages containing model evaluation outputs, risk register entries with confidential risk details, or vendor security assessments must be stored in access-controlled spaces. Not all programme staff need access to all gate packages — apply least-privilege access controls at the project space level.

Think of a milestone gate as a passport control checkpoint at an airport. You cannot proceed to the departure gate just because your flight is scheduled — you must present valid documentation (passport, boarding pass, security screening clearance) that is checked by an officer who has no stake in whether you catch your flight. If your documentation is incomplete, you are held at the checkpoint until it is resolved. The officer does not care about the inconvenience — their job is to ensure only properly credentialed passengers proceed.

Gate reviews work the same way. The panel's job is to check the evidence, not to be helpful to the delivery team. A gate panel that passes incomplete packages because 'the team is working hard' has abdicated its governance function and transferred the resulting risk to the organisation.
Gate reviews are evidence-based pass/fail checkpoints — not status meetings. The outcome must be one of: proceed, proceed with conditions, pause for remediation, or kill.
All six exit criteria sets must be defined before the project starts, not adapted to what the team delivered.
Panel independence is non-negotiable — reviewers must have no stake in the project's success.
The gate package (deliverables checklist, quality scorecard, risk register, budget actuals) must be distributed at least 5 business days before the review.
Time-box every review to a maximum of 2 hours — overruns signal an under-prepared package, not insufficient review time.
Gate 5 (Value Achieved) is as important as Gate 4 — without it, organisations never measure whether delivered AI features produced promised business outcomes.
A pharmaceutical company reduced post-launch failures by 40% using a 5-gate review process — the return on the governance overhead was decisive.
